text: Has this ever happened to you? You learn
many new words and when you see them,
you know what they mean, but when you
want to speak, you can't use them.
Of course. And
uh
this is a very common problem among
learners. In this video, I will help you
understand why it happens and how to fix
it. Understanding the problem. Let me
give you a very simple example. Look at
this guy. His name is Jack. He helps
everyone and everybody likes him. So,
let's describe Jack with a few
adjectives. He is a guy.
Mhm. Mhm. Did you say good or nice or
kind? All of these words are correct and
they're good. You thought about these
words because these words are active in
your brain and you can use them when you
want to speak. But instead of these
words, you could have said respectful,
reliable, trustworthy, wholesome,
well-mannered. Now, I'm pretty sure you
know the meaning of at least most of
these words, but when you wanted to
describe Jack, you didn't use any of
them. So, you know what they mean, but
you do not actively use them. That means
these words in your head are passive. So
words in your head are either active
that means you can use them when you
want to speak or passive which means if
you see them somewhere for example
you're reading a book and you see these
words you know what they mean but when
you want to speak you cannot use them.
But why does this happen? Why are the
words passive in your head? Think about
how you learn new words. Most students
learn words randomly. Let's watch a
movie and learn a few random words or
let's read this book and learn 20 words
today. But the thing is learning random
words doesn't help at all. In fact, it's
very bad for you. Let's say today you
learn four random words. Balance, visa,
office, recycle. Tomorrow you add four
new words. exercise, skill, map, waste.
And you keep adding more random words
every day. Shift, wildlife, ticket,
rest. Do you see what is happening in
your brain? Because all these words are
random. Your brain is not able to create
meaningful connections between these
words. It is not able to group them or
to categorize them. And eventually your
brain is not able to make sense of all
these random words you've learned. That
is why all these words are passive. If
you see them in a book or if you're
watching a movie and you hear one of
these words, you know what they mean
because they are in your head. But when
you want to speak, the random words are
all over the place. You cannot just
choose them and say anything you want.
That's why you know the meaning of
respectful or wholesome or
well-mannered. but instead you say good
or nice. So what's the solution? It's
very easy. You just have to turn the
passive words into active words. And
here are five things you need to do.
Step one, learn topic vocabulary. This
is very important. Instead of learning
random words, you need to have a topic
based approach. What does it mean? Let's
say you choose four topics. environment,
health and fitness, business English and
traveling. Then for each topic, you
learn some words related to that topic.
For example, for the environment, you
can say recycle, waste, wildlife. For
health and fitness, you can write
balance, exercise, and rest. For
business English, you can say office,
skill, and shift. And for traveling, you
can say visa, map, and ticket. Did you
see what happened in your mind? Instead
of having a mess with all those random
words you have learned over the years,
you now have clear folders, clear
categories of vocabulary. So the next
time there is a conversation about the
environment, your brain automatically
goes to the category of the environment
and all the words are already there.
Your brain is able to make sense of all
the relationships between these words.
Or if somebody asks you about your
health or fitness or sport activities,
your brain automatically goes to health
and fitness. This is the first and
probably one of the most important steps
towards speaking English fluently. And
that's exactly what we do at PC English

context. Here's the thing. Learning
single words never helps. For example,
look at the word change and look at
these two sentences. I need to change my
lifestyle. Do you have any change so I
can buy a bus ticket? In the first
sentence, change means to do something
differently. And in the second one, it
is a unit of currency. It's like asking
for smaller unit of money. Same words
but two different meanings and
technically two different parts of
speech. One of them is a verb and the
other one is a noun. Or here's another
example. Think about the word shift and
look at these three sentences. Can you
shift the table to the other side of the
room? He shifted to gear four and drove
faster. She was on a night shift. In the
first sentence, shift means move. In the
second one, it means change. And in the
third one, it's a period of work. Again,
same word, three meanings. You see what
I'm trying to say? It's very important
to see the word in context, to
understand its part of speech, whether
it's a noun or a verb, and to really
understand the meaning and how it is
used. So, learn words in sentences,
never alone. Step three, write a
paragraph. Now with all the words you
have learned, you're going to write one
paragraph and in this one paragraph you
will try to use as many of these words
as you can. Let's say I have learned a
few words about the topic environment.
These are the words that I have learned
waste. But remember you are not learning
the words alone. You need to see the
words in context. So we need a sentence.
We should reduce waste to protect the
planet. The second word is recycle. I
always recycle my plastic bottles.
Pollution. Air pollution is getting
worse in many big cities. Energy. We can
save energy by turning off the lights.
And finally, wildlife. Many types of
wildlife are in danger because of
humans. See, there is a clear topic and
there are some words in context. We have
a topic based approach. step one. And
we're seeing the words in sentences.
Step two. Now, in step three, we're
going to write a paragraph about this
topic using as many of these words as we
can. I want to help the earth. I try to
make less waste and I recycle plastic,
glass, and paper. This helps reduce
pollution. I also save energy when I
turn off the lights. When I do these
small things, I help protect wildlife.
Do not use AI to write this paragraph.
This is very important. That paragraph
needs to be produced by your brain. You
need to do it yourself. We need this.
After you write the paragraph, go to
Chad GPT, paste that paragraph and use
this prompt to make sure it's
grammatically accurate, but only after
writing the paragraph. Here's the
prompt. Check this sentence for grammar
or vocabulary mistakes. Highlight
mistakes. Very important. Suggest
corrections and explain why they are
wrong. Use the prompt after you have
written the text yourself, not before.
So there you have it. You have one
topic, the environment. You have five
words related to that topic. Each word
comes with at least one sentence. So we
can see that word in context. And
finally, we have written a paragraph in
which we have used all those words. Step
four, review the words by reading your
paragraph. Learning words is one thing.
Reviewing them is another thing. How can
you review the words? What one of the
options you have is just to review the
single words, but that's not going to
help. What's the best way to review
these words? You can read the words
alone. You can read the sentences that
you wrote for every word, but the best
way to review the words is to read your
own paragraph. Remember the paragraph
you wrote about each topic. Go ahead,
read that paragraph again and again and
review the words using that one. But why
does this help? That paragraph was
produced by your brain. Remember I said
do not use AI and it has to come from
you. That paragraph was produced by your
brain. So when you read it again, it
sounds familiar. It is no longer a new
context. It's the context you created
yourself and you're seeing the new words
in your own context. That is why it's
extremely effective to read that
paragraph and review the words again. If
